items.q: Is the manager the person who does the most work in a team?
items.correct: Not necessarily — managers plan, organise, support and check the team's work; their job is making others effective rather than doing every task themselves.
items.misconception: Managers are just the busiest workers.
items.random_wrong: Managers are chosen because they are the tallest.
items.why: Understanding the coordinating role of management is the basis for learning organisational structure.

items.q: Do directors and managers do the same job?
items.correct: No — directors set the long-term direction and major decisions of the business, while managers organise the day-to-day work needed to deliver those plans.
items.misconception: Director is just a fancier word for manager.
items.why: Levels of hierarchy confuse students; separating strategic from operational roles fixes it.

items.q: Are front-line staff less important than office staff?
items.correct: No — front-line staff deal directly with customers and shape the firm's reputation, while back-office roles support them; both are needed for the business to work.
items.misconception: Jobs you can't see, like back-office work, matter more than serving customers.
items.random_wrong: Front-line staff are soldiers employed by shops.
items.why: Valuing different roles helps students map how departments work together.
It's Money. “Adverts” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Correct answer: Meet customers. “Hide from customers” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A shop has 1 director, 4 managers, 35 staff. Total people?
items.why: Exact answer: 40. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
